name: John Wilkes Booth
description: John Wilkes Booth (1838-1865) was an American actor and Confederate sympathizer who assassinated President Abraham Lincoln on April 14, 1865. While his role in Lincoln's assassination is a well-established historical fact, various conspiracy theories and alternative historical claims surround his death and alleged motivations. Some theories suggest Booth was part of a larger conspiracy involving high-ranking Confederate officials or even members of Lincoln's own cabinet, aiming to destabilize the Union government. Proponents of these theories often cite inconsistencies in the official accounts of Booth's escape and death, questioning the identity of the man killed at Garrett's Farm. Evidence cited often includes supposed eyewitness accounts, discrepancies in physical descriptions, and the perceived lack of a thorough investigation. Other theories claim Booth escaped capture and lived under an assumed identity, possibly fathering children. Mainstream historical perspectives largely refute these claims, citing overwhelming evidence supporting Booth's sole responsibility for the assassination and his subsequent death at Garrett's Farm. Historians emphasize the reliability of eyewitness testimonies, forensic evidence, and the lack of credible evidence supporting an escape. They attribute the enduring conspiracy theories to the trauma of Lincoln's assassination and the desire to find a deeper, more sinister explanation for such a pivotal event. The romanticized image of Booth, a charismatic actor, also contributes to the allure of alternative narratives. Scholarly consensus points to Booth's deep-seated racism and fervent support for the Confederacy as the primary motivations for his actions.
subtitle: Assassin or scapegoat? The conspiracies.

claims.claim: Booth was part of a larger Confederate conspiracy.
claims.evidence: Alleged connections to Confederate agents, inconsistencies in witness testimonies regarding the escape plot.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence linking high-ranking Confederate officials to the assassination plot. Inconsistencies are attributed to the chaos and confusion of the aftermath.

claims.claim: Booth escaped capture and lived under an assumed identity.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts claiming the man killed at Garrett's Farm was not Booth, alleged sightings of Booth after his supposed death.
claims.criticism: Forensic evidence and the overwhelming majority of eyewitness accounts confirm the man killed was Booth. Sightings are unsubstantiated and unreliable.

claims.claim: Members of Lincoln's cabinet were involved in the assassination.
claims.evidence: Suggestions of motives for removing Lincoln, perceived lack of thorough investigation into potential co-conspirators.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence implicates any members of Lincoln's cabinet. The investigation was extensive, though imperfect, given the historical context.

keyFigures.name: James Swanson
keyFigures.role: Historian providing detailed account of the assassination and Booth's pursuit based on factual evidence.

timeline.event: John Wilkes Booth assassinates Abraham Lincoln at Ford's Theatre.
Booth is cornered and killed at Garrett's Farm in Virginia.
Emergence of alternative theories surrounding Booth's death and the assassination plot.

sources.author: Swanson, James L.
sources.work: Manhunt: The 12-Day Chase for Lincoln's Killer
sources.title: Swanson, James L.

sources.author: Kauffman, Michael W.
sources.work: American Brutus: John Wilkes Booth and the Lincoln Conspiracies
sources.title: Kauffman, Michael W.
